{{Short description="Integer or half-integer that describes quantized properties of particles and systems}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Quantum number
| image = 
| caption = 
| topics = [[Quantum mechanics]], [[Atomic physics]], [[Schrödinger equation]], [[Wave function]]
}}

In [[quantum mechanics]], '''quantum numbers''' are discrete ([[quantization (physics)|quantized]]) values that characterise the state of a quantum system. They arise as [[eigenvalue]]s of [[operator (physics)|operators]] associated with conserved quantities and physical [[observable]]s.

== Atomic quantum numbers ==
For an [[electron]] in an [[atom]], four quantum numbers describe the complete state:

=== Principal quantum number (<math>n</math>) ===
:<math>n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots</math>
Determines the energy and size of the orbital. For [[hydrogen]]:
:<math>E_n = -\frac{13.6\ \text{eV}}{n^2}</math>

=== Azimuthal (orbital) quantum number (<math>\ell</math>) ===
:<math>\ell = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n-1</math>
Determines the orbital angular momentum:
:<math>L^2 = \ell(\ell+1)\hbar^2</math>
Notation: s (ℓ=0), p (ℓ=1), d (ℓ=2), f (ℓ=3)

=== Magnetic quantum number (<math>m_\ell</math>) ===
:<math>m_\ell = -\ell, -\ell+1, \ldots, +\ell</math>
Determines the z-component of orbital angular momentum:
:<math>L_z = m_\ell\hbar</math>

=== Spin quantum number (<math>m_s</math>) ===
:<math>m_s = \pm \frac{1}{2}</math> (for electrons)
Determines the z-component of spin angular momentum:
:<math>S_z = m_s\hbar</math>

== Other quantum numbers ==
* '''Isospin''' — related to the [[strong interaction]] symmetry between protons and neutrons
* '''[[Strangeness]]''' — quantum number for strange quarks
* '''[[Charm (quantum number)|Charm]], [[Bottomness]], [[Topness]]''' — for heavier quarks
* '''[[Baryon number]]''' — conserved in the Standard Model
* '''[[Lepton number]]''' — conserved separately for each lepton generation
* '''[[Colour charge]]''' — red, green, blue for quarks

== Pauli exclusion principle ==
{{Main|Pauli exclusion principle}}
No two [[fermion]]s can occupy the same quantum state. For electrons in atoms, this means no two electrons can have the same set of four quantum numbers <math>(n, \ell, m_\ell, m_s)</math>.

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Atomic orbital]]
* [[Atomic physics]]
* [[Energy level]]
* [[Pauli exclusion principle]]
* [[Quantization (physics)]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Quantum mechanics]]
[[Category:Atomic physics]]
